narrative:
Let's take a closer look at the prediction that your application was rejected because the model anticipated you might not pass the bar exam on your first attempt. This prediction was based on several factors from your academic and personal background. Your current profile shows that your grades in Year 3, LSAT score, and undergraduate GPA were below the average of other students who typically pass the exam. Specifically, your Year 3 decile was significantly lower than average, and your LSAT score and GPA were also slightly below the norm.

Counterfactuals are essentially "what if" scenarios that help us understand what changes could lead to a different prediction. They show us the smallest adjustments needed in your profile to flip the prediction from "fail" to "pass." In your case, the model generated several counterfactual scenarios, each suggesting different combinations of changes that could improve your likelihood of passing.

From these scenarios, we see that your undergraduate GPA is a critical factor. In every scenario where the prediction changed to "pass," your GPA was slightly higher than it currently is. This suggests that improving your GPA could significantly impact the model's prediction. Additionally, in some scenarios, a slight increase in your LSAT score or a change in your Year 3 decile also contributed to a positive prediction.

Interestingly, some features, like your family income and whether you work full-time, did not change in any of the scenarios, indicating they are less influential in altering the prediction. The most effective pattern observed was a combination of a higher GPA with either a slight improvement in your LSAT score or an increase in your Year 3 decile.

To improve your chances of passing the bar exam and having your application approved, focusing on raising your undergraduate GPA would be a practical step. Even a modest increase could make a difference. Additionally, if feasible, consider strategies to slightly boost your LSAT score or improve your academic performance in your final year. These changes, while challenging, are actionable and could significantly enhance your profile in the eyes of the model.
